title: Isaiah 23
subtitle: Oracle Against Tyre

heb.paragraph: "Wail (hēlîlū), you ships of Tarshish! For Tyre is destroyed and left without house or harbour" (v.1). Tyre, the great Phoenician trading port. The ships returning from Tarshish (Spain) learn the news at Cyprus (v.1). The grain of the Nile, the harvest of the Shihor, was Tyre’s revenue (v.3). The sea speaks: "I have neither been in labour nor given birth; I have neither reared sons nor brought up daughters" (v.4) — the sea personified as barren, having produced nothing lasting. "Who planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns?" (v.8). Answer: "The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory" (v.9).

notes.note: "Wail, ships of Tarshish!" The Mediterranean trade network collapses. The news arrives at Cyprus: Tyre is gone.
"Who planned this against Tyre? The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory." The theology of every nation-oracle condensed: human pride, divine plan.

refs.note: Ezekiel's extended oracle against Tyre expands on Isaiah's themes: pride, commerce, and destruction by Nebuchadnezzar.
Amos condemns Tyre for slave-trading and breaking treaty obligations with Israel.
Zechariah predicts Tyre's fall to Alexander the Great, fulfilled in 332 BC.
Babylon the Great's fall echoes Tyre's commercial hubris and sudden destruction.

hist.context: Tyre was the premier Phoenician city-state, a maritime trading empire whose ships reached Spain. Its island fortress seemed impregnable. Isaiah's oracle likely dates to the Assyrian campaigns of Sennacherib (701 BC) or earlier. The 'seventy years' (v.15) may parallel Jeremiah's seventy-year exile prediction. Tyre's pride in self-sufficiency and commercial wealth made it a paradigm of human arrogance against divine sovereignty.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt observes that Tyre's ships discover the disaster only when they reach port — the news travels faster by land. The 'ships of Tarshish' (v.1) represent the far-flung commercial network now disrupted.
The key theological verse: 'The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory.' Tyre's fall is not accident but divine intention against human self-exaltation.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the Tyre oracle as concluding the oracles against nations (chs.13-23). Tyre represents commercial civilization's claim to autonomy; its fall demonstrates that economic power cannot secure against divine judgment.
The restoration promise (v.18) is striking: Tyre's wealth will eventually serve 'those who live before the LORD.' Commerce itself is not condemned, only commerce divorced from divine purpose.

sections.header: Verses 10–18 — “After Seventy Years, the LORD Will Deal with Tyre”

heb.paragraph: "Her profit and her earnings will be set apart (qōdesh) for the LORD; they will not be stored up or hoarded. Her profits will go to those who live before the LORD" (v.18). After seventy years (v.15) — the same period as Judah’s exile — Tyre will return to trade. But the stunning twist: Tyre’s profits will be consecrated to the LORD. Commercial wealth redirected to holy purposes. The pagan trading empire becomes a supplier for God’s people. Like Egypt (ch.19) and Cush (ch.18), the enemy’s resources are repurposed for God’s kingdom.

notes.note: "At the end of seventy years, the LORD will deal with Tyre. She will return to her lucrative prostitution." Tyre’s trade is described as prostitution — selling herself to all comers. Revelation 17–18 develops this Tyre/Babylon-as-prostitute imagery.
"Her profit will be set apart for the LORD." The surprise: not destruction but consecration. Pagan wealth redirected to holy use. The pattern: judgment → restoration → service to God.

refs.note: The daughter of Tyre bringing gifts — Tyre's wealth in service to God's king.
Hiram of Tyre provided materials for Solomon's temple — Phoenician commerce serving sacred purposes.
Ezekiel's lament over Tyre catalogs its trade goods and trading partners in extraordinary detail.

hist.context: The 'seventy years' (v.15) suggests a generation of obscurity before restoration. Tyre did survive Assyrian and Babylonian sieges, only to fall definitively to Alexander who built a causeway to the island. The 'song of the prostitute' (v.16) uses commercial metaphor: Tyre will return to 'business as usual,' but ultimately her profits will serve Yahweh's people. This redemption of pagan commerce anticipates the nations bringing their wealth to Zion (60:5-7).

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the prostitute metaphor: Tyre's commerce is promiscuous — she trades with anyone for profit. The seventy years parallel Babylon's judgment period in Jeremiah.
The surprising conclusion: Tyre's wealth will be 'holy to the LORD.' Oswalt sees eschatological hope: even pagan commerce will be transformed to serve divine purposes in the age to come.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs emphasizes the canonical function: the oracles against nations (13-23) end not with destruction but with transformed commerce serving Zion. This anticipates chapters 60-62 where nations bring tribute to Jerusalem. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "Who planned this against Tyre? The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVOn the great waters came the grain of the Shihor; the harvest of the Nile was the revenue of Tyre, and she became the marketplace of the nations.ESVAnd on many waters your revenue was the grain of Shihor, the harvest of the Nile; you were the merchant of the nations.NIVWho planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns, whose merchants are princes, whose traders are renowned in the earth?ESVWho has purposed this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns, whose merchants were princes, whose traders were the honored of the earth?

rec.text: ""Wail, ships of Tarshish!" The Mediterranean trade network collapses. The news arrives at Cyprus: Tyre is gone."

rows.label: Pride Brought Down — 23:1–9
rows.text: "Wail, ships!" "The LORD planned it, to bring down the PRIDE of all glory."

hebtext.note: "Wail (hēlîlū), you ships of Tarshish! For Tyre is destroyed and left without house or harbour" (v.1). Tyre, the great Phoenician trading port. The ships returning from Tarshish (Spain) learn the news at Cyprus (v.1). The grain of the Nile, the harvest of the Shihor, was Tyre’s revenue (v.3). The sea speaks: "I have neither been in labour nor given birth; I have neither reared sons nor brought up daughters" (v.4) — the sea personified as barren, having produced nothing lasting. "Who planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns?" (v.8). Answer: "The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory" (v.9).

"Her profit and her earnings will be set apart (qōdesh) for the LORD; they will not be stored up or hoarded. Her profits will go to those who live before the LORD" (v.18). After seventy years (v.15) — the same period as Judah’s exile — Tyre will return to trade. But the stunning twist: Tyre’s profits will be consecrated to the LORD. Commercial wealth redirected to holy purposes. The pagan trading empire becomes a supplier for God’s people. Like Egypt (ch.19) and Cush (ch.18), the enemy’s resources are repurposed for God’s kingdom.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: God plans Tyre’s fall and Tyre’s consecration. The oracles-against-nations section ends with the pattern complete: judgment, then surprising inclusion.

coaching.tip: The Tyre oracle mourns a city defined by commerce — 'whose merchants are princes, whose traders are renowned in the earth' (v. 8). Tyre represents a civilization built entirely on economic power. Ezekiel 27–28 will develop this theme at length, and Revelation 18 models 'Babylon the Great' on this same mercantile template. The prophets consistently warn that wealth-as-identity is uniquely fragile.

After seventy years, Tyre's wealth will be 'set apart for the LORD' (v. 18) — her profits will 'supply abundant food and fine clothes for those who live before the LORD.' Judgment doesn't annihilate Tyre's commercial gifts but redirects them. Isaiah envisions redemption of vocation, not its elimination. Even pagan commerce can serve God's purposes once rightly ordered.

prayer_prompt: As Tyre's commercial empire is humbled and then eventually redirected for holy purposes, consider asking God whether your professional gifts and resources are ultimately serving His kingdom or building your own.
